title: Simple Delay
summary: Tempo-synced delay that creates rhythmic echoes locked to your groove. Route via a return track. Dotted 1/8 delay is the signature UK garage pad echo feel.
steps: Create a return track and add Simple Delay to it (same approach as Reverb — always a return, not an insert)
Add EQ Eight before Simple Delay on the return track: high-pass at 250Hz to keep delay tails clear
Set Sync mode: click the note icon to enable tempo sync on L and R channels
Set delay time: 1/8 = eighth-note delay. Dotted 1/8 = the classic garage pad echo (adds rhythmic interest without crowding the beat). 1/4 = quarter-note echo
Set Feedback: how many times the echo repeats. 25–35% = 2–3 audible repeats then fades. 50%+ = many repeating echoes
On the source track, turn up the Send knob for this return to taste — usually -14 to -10dB for a subtle delay tail
tips: Dotted 1/8 is the most useful delay time for UK garage pads — it creates echoes that land between the beats, adding rhythmic interest without clashing with the kick
Ping-pong delay (L and R at different times) creates movement across the stereo field — try L at 1/8, R at 1/4 for an asymmetric ping-pong
High-pass the delay return at 250Hz: low frequencies in the delay tails create mud fast. The brightness and upper harmonics are what you want to echo
Delay and reverb can share the same return track, or live on separate returns. Separate returns give more control — recommended
practice_hint: In any spinoff session with a chord pad, create a return track with Simple Delay, set Dotted 1/8 sync, Feedback 30%, HP at 250Hz. Send the pad at -12dB. Toggle the send on and off to hear the difference.
ableton_version_note: Simple Delay available in all editions of Live 9+. Echo (a more advanced delay) is available in Live 11 Suite only.
